intro.title: Welcome to the SOC
intro.body: A Security Operations Center is where cybersecurity teams monitor a network in real-time and respond to attacks as they happen. You're about to observe one in action.
tips: The feed on the right lists live threats. Click any one to learn what it is.
The team chat shows how analysts coordinate during an incident.
The dashboards below track traffic, attack types, and system health.
intro.goal: Your goal: follow along, click on threats you don't recognize, and see how a real team handles them.

traffic.title: Network Traffic
traffic.explanation: This graph shows the volume of network packets over time. Each bar represents a time interval. Spikes indicate unusual activity — a sudden surge could mean a DDoS attack, data exfiltration, or a worm scanning the network.
traffic.why: In a real SOC, tools like Wireshark, Zeek, and NetFlow capture and visualize network traffic. Anomaly detection algorithms flag deviations from baseline patterns automatically.

attacks.title: Attack Types
attacks.explanation: This chart breaks down detected threats by category. If phishing suddenly dominates, it might indicate a targeted campaign. If DDoS spikes, infrastructure is under pressure.
attacks.why: Real SOCs use SIEM systems (Splunk, QRadar, Sentinel) to categorize and count alerts. This helps prioritize response.

severity.title: Severity Classification
severity.explanation: Every alert is classified by severity: LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, CRITICAL. The donut chart shows the distribution. Too many CRITICAL alerts means the organization is under active attack.
severity.why: Severity classification follows standards like CVSS. It determines response priority — CRITICAL alerts wake people up at 3 AM.

status.title: System Health
status.explanation: This panel monitors key security infrastructure: Firewall, IDS/IPS, SIEM, and Endpoints. Green means operational. Yellow means degraded. Red means down.
status.why: If the firewall goes down, attackers can walk right in. SOC teams monitor these 24/7.

chat.title: Team Chat
chat.explanation: In a real SOC, analysts communicate constantly during incidents. Each team member has a role: Threat Detection, Risk Assessment, Forensics, and Incident Response.
chat.why: Communication is critical during incidents. Miscommunication can turn a contained threat into a full breach.

intro.title: Rootkit
intro.description: You'll see a normal-looking Task Manager. Everything seems fine — low CPU, familiar processes. Then reveal what the rootkit is hiding from you.
intro.disclaimer: 🔒 No system processes are scanned. The Task Manager is a visual mockup with fake data.

lesson.title: The Invisible Threat
lesson.body: Rootkits operate at the kernel level, hooking into the OS to hide processes, files, and network connections from all user-mode tools — including Task Manager. Your CPU was at 86% but you only saw 8%. Defense: Use offline scanning tools, boot from clean media, enable Secure Boot and UEFI protections, and monitor from an external system.

reveal.title: Everything Was Captured
reveal.lessonBody: A keylogger records every single keystroke — including passwords you type, messages you delete, and corrections you make. Even if you backspace over a mistake, the original characters are already captured. Defense: Use password managers (they autofill without typing), enable 2FA, keep antivirus updated, and never install software from untrusted sources.

crashed.title: System Overwhelmed
crashed.description: The virus replicated itself by opening new processes in an infinite loop. Each process spawned more processes until the system ran out of memory — just like a real fork bomb.
crashed.howBody:
1. The script opens a new command window
2. Then loops back and does it again
3. Each new window does the same thing
4. Exponential growth: 1 → 2 → 4 → 8 → 16 → 32...
5. System runs out of memory and crashes
crashed.lesson: Lesson: Never open suspicious files! Even a tiny script can crash an entire computer through self-replication.

lesson.body: Ransomware uses a Command & Control (C2) server to remotely lock victims' screens. The attacker can lock/unlock at will.
lesson.defenseBody: Regular backups, network segmentation, and endpoint detection stop ransomware before it activates.

lesson.label: Polymorphic malware
lesson.body: encrypts its payload with a new key each time. The hash changes every generation, but the decrypted payload is always identical.

lesson.label: Metamorphic malware
lesson.body: rewrites its own code — different instructions, registers, and structure — but the behavior is always identical. No encryption needed. Nearly impossible to detect with signatures.

scenarios.speaker: xDarkSlayer
scenarios.msg: Yo you're so good! What's your real name? I wanna add you on Insta

opts.text: Thanks! I'm Ahmed, find me @ahmed_2010
opts.tip: Never share your real name or personal social media with strangers in games — you don't know who they really are!

opts.text: Thanks! You can add my gaming tag: CyberFalcon22
opts.tip: Perfect! Share gaming identities, not real ones. Keep your real life completely separate from gaming.

opts.text: I don't want to talk to you
opts.tip: Being rude isn't necessary. Just redirect to your gaming identity — friendly AND safe.

scenarios.speaker: Unknown Number
scenarios.msg: Hey everyone! I'm doing a school survey. Can you share your age, neighborhood, and school name?

opts.text: Sure! I'm 15, I live in Al Wakrah, I go to...
opts.tip: Never share personal details with unknown people — even in 'surveys'. This info can be used to locate you.

opts.text: Who are you? I don't share personal info with people I don't know.
opts.tip: Always question who's asking and why. Real surveys don't collect this via WhatsApp groups from unknown numbers.

opts.text: [Leave the group silently]
opts.tip: Leaving is okay, but it's better to warn others in the group too so they don't fall for it!

scenarios.speaker: ProBuilder_2024
scenarios.msg: I can give you unlimited Robux! Just tell me your password and I'll add them to your account 💎

opts.text: Really?! My password is Falcon123
opts.tip: NEVER share your password! Nobody can 'add Robux' by knowing your password — this is a classic credential theft scam.

opts.text: No thanks. No one needs my password to give me anything.
opts.tip: Exactly right! Roblox says NEVER share your password. Real rewards don't require your credentials.

opts.text: How about you give me your password first?
opts.tip: Don't try to counter-scam — just refuse and report the user to Roblox support.

scenarios.speaker: Fashion_Brand_QA
scenarios.msg: We love your profile! We want to pay you QAR 5,000 to promote our brand. Send us your bank details for payment 🤩

opts.text: OMG yes! Here's my bank info...
opts.tip: Legitimate brands NEVER ask for bank details via DM. This is the most common influencer scam.

opts.text: Sounds interesting! Send me your official website so I can verify first.
opts.tip: Smart! Always verify the brand independently before sharing anything. Real brands have verifiable websites and official email domains.

opts.text: Send me the money first as proof
opts.tip: Don't engage with potential scammers on their terms. Verify the brand independently without giving them anything.

scenarios.speaker: Admin_Steve
scenarios.msg: Your account has been flagged for hacking. Give me your login email and password or you'll be banned.

opts.text: Please don't ban me! My email is... and password is...
opts.tip: Real admins NEVER need your password. They have server-side tools and logs. This is an authority impersonation scam.

opts.text: Real admins don't need my password. I'm reporting this.
opts.tip: Correct! Any platform's staff can act without your password. Anyone asking is impersonating authority to steal credentials.

opts.text: [Ignore the message]
opts.tip: Ignoring protects you, but reporting is better so they don't scam other players on the same server.

scenarios.speaker: NitroGiveaway_Bot
scenarios.msg: 🎉 Free Discord Nitro! Click here to claim: discord-nitro-free.xyz/claim

opts.text: [Click the link excitedly]
opts.tip: Fake Nitro links steal your Discord token — giving attackers full control of your account. Real Nitro only comes through the official Discord app.

opts.text: That's a scam link. Real Nitro is only through discord.com. Reported.
opts.tip: Perfect! The domain 'discord-nitro-free.xyz' is NOT discord.com. Always check the URL. When in doubt, go directly to the official site.

opts.text: Is this real? I'll ask my friends first
opts.tip: The safest approach is to assume external links offering free things are fake. Verify by going directly to discord.com, not clicking the link.

scenarios.speaker: cute_stranger_94
scenarios.msg: Hey! You seem really cool 😊 Where do you go to school? What part of Doha do you live in?

opts.text: I go to QU! I live near City Center Doha
opts.tip: This combination of info — school + neighborhood — helps someone locate you in real life. Never share both with strangers.

opts.text: I'm not comfortable sharing that with someone I don't know yet.
opts.tip: Exactly right. These two pieces of info together can identify your daily location. Trust is built over time, not in first messages.

opts.text: Why do you want to know? Are you a stalker? 😂
opts.tip: Joking about it still keeps the conversation going. It's better to clearly decline and not share the information.

scenarios.speaker: VoicePlayer_77
scenarios.msg: Hey add me on WhatsApp! My number is +974... and tell me yours, easier to coordinate!

opts.text: Sure! Mine is +974...
opts.tip: Never share your phone number with strangers in games. Phone numbers can be used for SIM swapping, spam, and social engineering.

opts.text: Let's just use the in-game chat — no need for WhatsApp!
opts.tip: Keep gaming on gaming platforms. In-game chat is designed for this — no need to exchange personal contact info with strangers.

opts.text: [Stay silent and don't respond]
opts.tip: Silence works, but a clear polite decline is better so there's no awkwardness and you can still play together.

scenarios.speaker: IT Support <it-support@sch00l.com>
scenarios.msg: Dear Student, your account will expire. Click to verify: http://school-portal-verify.net/login — IT Department

opts.text: [Click the link and enter school credentials]
opts.tip: The sender domain is 'sch00l.com' (zeros instead of o's) and the link goes to an external site. This is a phishing email targeting students.

opts.text: This looks suspicious — I'll go directly to the official school portal instead.
opts.tip: Never click links in emails for login. Always type the official school URL directly in your browser. Real IT departments use official school domains.

opts.text: Reply asking if it's legitimate
opts.tip: Replying to a phishing email confirms your address is active. Go directly to the official portal instead of interacting with the suspicious email.

scenarios.speaker: Investment_Advisor_Official
scenarios.msg: Special offer for QU students! Invest QAR 200 and earn QAR 2,000 in 7 days. 100% guaranteed. Send to: QR123456

opts.text: This sounds amazing! Let me send the QAR 200 now
opts.tip: If an investment 'guarantees' 10x returns in 7 days, it is 100% a scam. No legitimate investment works this way.

opts.text: This is a scam. No investment guarantees 10x returns. Reporting and leaving.
opts.tip: Correct! Guaranteed high returns in short periods = classic Ponzi/advance fee fraud. Real investments have risk. Report and leave.

opts.text: I'll invest only QAR 50 to test it first
opts.tip: Even testing a scam sends your money to criminals. The 'small test' is designed to build your trust before taking more.

scenarios.speaker: PS_Support_Official
scenarios.msg: Your PS account has suspicious activity. Verify your credit card details here to keep your account: psn-verify.co/secure

opts.text: Better safe than sorry — I'll verify my card details
opts.tip: PlayStation Support never contacts you via chat asking for card details. The domain 'psn-verify.co' is not playstation.com.

opts.text: I'll go to playstation.com directly and check my account from there.
opts.tip: Always navigate directly to official sites for account issues. Real platform support never asks for card details via chat messages.

opts.text: I'll ask my parents to deal with this
opts.tip: That's better than clicking, but still — go directly to playstation.com. Don't click the link at all.

scenarios.speaker: TikTok_Verified_Team
scenarios.msg: Congratulations! Your account has been selected for Verification ✓. Send us your phone number and date of birth to proceed.

opts.text: Amazing! My number is +974... and I was born on...
opts.tip: TikTok verification is never given out via DM. This info (phone + DOB) is used for identity theft and account takeover.

opts.text: TikTok never verifies accounts through DMs. This is a scam. Blocking.
opts.tip: Correct! All legitimate platform verifications happen through official in-app processes, never via DM requests for personal info.

opts.text: Can you prove you're really TikTok staff?
opts.tip: Scammers can easily fake 'proof'. The safest response is to block and report, not engage in verification attempts.

intro.line1: You're the network firewall! Data packets stream toward your server — you decide what gets blocked.
intro.line2: TAP a packet to block it. Green flash = correct block. Red flash = you blocked legitimate traffic!
intro.line3: Careful — some threats disguise themselves as normal traffic. Read the details, not just the label!
intro.line4: At the end you'll see a review of every mistake so you can learn from it.

review.title: Session Review
review.subtitle: Here's what happened this round
review.mistakesHeader: ✗ {count} false block(s) — you blocked legitimate traffic:
review.xpRequirement: Need {passingScore} points to earn XP. You scored {score}.

packetPool.label: 🦠 Trojan.exe
packetPool.hint: Executable files from unknown sources carry malware — never open .exe attachments.

packetPool.label: 📡 C2 Beacon
packetPool.hint: Port 4444 is a classic command-and-control channel used by malware to phone home.

packetPool.label: 💉 SQL injection
packetPool.hint: SQL injection attempts to destroy or steal database data by manipulating queries.

packetPool.label: 🌊 DDoS flood
packetPool.hint: No legitimate user sends 50,000 requests per second — this is a denial-of-service attack.

packetPool.label: 🔍 Port scan
packetPool.hint: Scanning all ports is reconnaissance — the attacker is mapping your attack surface.

packetPool.label: 📄 File download
packetPool.hint: Normal HTTP file request from an authenticated user.

packetPool.label: 📧 Email send
packetPool.hint: Authenticated email via the official company mail server on the correct port.

packetPool.label: 🌐 DNS query
packetPool.hint: Normal domain name resolution — looking up a well-known legitimate domain.

packetPool.label: 🔑 Login request
packetPool.hint: Normal authentication attempt with correct credentials from a known IP.

packetPool.label: 📦 Windows Update
packetPool.hint: Official Microsoft update server on the real microsoft.com domain.

packetPool.label: 📄 Invoice.pdf.exe
packetPool.hint: Double extension trick — Windows hides the .exe part. This is malware disguised as a PDF!

packetPool.label: 🌐 DNS query
packetPool.hint: Typosquatted domain — 'g00gle' uses the number zero, not the letter O. Leads to a fake site!

packetPool.label: 🔑 Login request
packetPool.hint: Attempt #4,847 is a brute-force attack, not a real user. Real users don't fail thousands of times.

packetPool.label: 📦 Software update
packetPool.hint: Fake update server! Real Microsoft updates come from microsoft.com, not microsft.co or similar.

packetPool.label: 📧 System email
packetPool.hint: Zero instead of 'o' — a spoofed sender domain designed to look legitimate at a glance.

packetPool.label: 🌐 API call
packetPool.hint: SQL injection hiding inside an API request parameter — the 'OR 1=1' is the attack.

packetPool.label: 🔐 Encrypted traffic
packetPool.hint: Encrypted internal traffic is normal and expected — encryption protects legitimate data too.

packetPool.label: 📡 Large upload
packetPool.hint: Large uploads to an internal backup server are completely legitimate maintenance.

packetPool.label: 🕐 Late-night login
packetPool.hint: Known IP address means it's the same authorized person, just working late.

packetPool.label: 🔧 Admin command
packetPool.sublabel: sudo apt-get update — IT dept IP
packetPool.hint: IT department running system updates from their known IP is normal maintenance.

intro.line1: Ransomware has struck! Your files are held hostage by attackers demanding Bitcoin.
intro.line2: You'll face a real-world scenario — hospital, university, small business, or home — and make decisions at each step.
intro.line3: Every choice matters. The right decisions save your data. The wrong ones make things worse. Don't panic — think!

result.xpNeeded: Need {passingScore}/5 correct decisions to earn XP. You got {score}/5.
result.perfectSub: Every decision was correct. You're ready for the real thing.

scenarios.title: Hospital Ransomware Attack
scenarios.banner: CRITICAL: Patient records encrypted! Pay 10 BTC within 72 hours or data will be deleted.

steps.q: Your hospital's screens show a ransomware message. Patient records are inaccessible. What's your FIRST action?

opts.text: Disconnect infected systems from the network immediately
opts.tip: Correct! Isolation prevents the ransomware from spreading to other hospital systems — including life-critical equipment.

opts.text: Pay the 10 BTC ransom to restore access quickly
opts.tip: Never pay first! There's no guarantee attackers will restore access, and it funds more attacks.

opts.text: Reboot all computers to clear the ransomware
opts.tip: Rebooting doesn't remove ransomware and may trigger additional encryption or delete recovery evidence.

steps.q: Systems isolated. The attackers claim to have stolen patient data too. What do you do?

opts.text: Notify authorities and your legal/compliance team immediately
opts.tip: Data breaches involving patient records legally require notification. Authorities can also assist with investigation and may have decryption keys from previous cases.

opts.text: Keep it quiet to avoid bad press
opts.tip: Hiding a data breach is illegal in most jurisdictions and causes far worse reputational damage when discovered later.

opts.text: Negotiate with attackers to prevent data release
opts.tip: Negotiating often escalates demands. Authorities must be involved for patient data breaches regardless.

steps.q: IT found offline backups from 2 days ago. How should you restore?

opts.text: Scan and verify backups are clean, then restore to fresh systems
opts.tip: Always verify backups before restoring! Restoring to a clean system prevents reinfection from any lingering malware.

opts.text: Restore directly to the infected machines to save time
opts.tip: Restoring to infected machines risks reinfection. Always restore to freshly imaged systems.

opts.text: Just pay the ransom — restoring takes too long
opts.tip: Restoration from clean backups, though slower, is always safer and doesn't fund criminals.

steps.q: After recovery, your CEO asks: 'How did they get in?' Investigation shows a phishing email opened 3 weeks ago. What do you implement?

opts.text: Mandatory phishing awareness training + email filtering + MFA on all systems
opts.tip: The triple defense: trained staff catch phishing, email filters block it, and MFA ensures one compromised password can't compromise systems.

opts.text: Just buy a more expensive antivirus
opts.tip: Antivirus alone doesn't stop phishing-based attacks. Human training is equally critical.

opts.text: Ban all external emails
opts.tip: Blocking all external email cripples operations. Filtering and training are the practical solutions.

steps.q: For future ransomware prevention, what backup strategy should the hospital adopt?

opts.text: 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 different media, 1 offsite air-gapped backup
opts.tip: The 3-2-1 rule is the gold standard! Air-gapped (offline) backups are immune to ransomware because they can't be reached over the network.

opts.text: Sync everything to cloud storage automatically
opts.tip: Cloud sync can sync the encrypted files too! You need versioned backups with rollback capability, not just sync.

opts.text: Back up weekly — that's enough
opts.tip: Weekly backups mean up to 7 days of patient data lost. Critical systems should back up daily or more frequently.

scenarios.title: University Student Ransomware
scenarios.banner: YOUR FILES ARE ENCRYPTED! Pay 0.5 BTC to recover your thesis and all assignments.

steps.q: Your laptop screen shows a ransom note. Your thesis is due in 2 days. What do you do first?

opts.text: Disconnect from WiFi and unplug any USB drives
opts.tip: Disconnect immediately! This stops the ransomware from spreading to shared university network drives or backing up its own files.

opts.text: Pay 0.5 BTC — it's cheaper than redoing the thesis
opts.tip: Don't pay! Many victims pay and receive nothing. Check backups first.

opts.text: Email your supervisor for an extension
opts.tip: That can come later — first priority is containing the damage, not explaining it.

steps.q: You're disconnected. Where's the first place you should look for your thesis?

opts.text: OneDrive / Google Drive — cloud-synced copies
opts.tip: Cloud drives with version history often have copies of files before encryption! Check version history immediately.

opts.text: The Downloads folder on the infected laptop
opts.tip: Downloaded files on the infected machine are likely encrypted too.

opts.text: Ask a classmate if they have a copy
opts.tip: That's a last resort — check your own cloud backups first.

steps.q: OneDrive has a version from yesterday! But the ransomware note warns: 'Do NOT restore or we delete your data.' What do you do?

opts.text: Restore from OneDrive version history — the threat is a bluff
opts.tip: Ransomware notes often threaten to prevent you from restoring. In most cases it's a bluff — restore your backups immediately!

opts.text: Pay first, then restore
opts.tip: Paying doesn't guarantee decryption. You have a clean backup — use it!

opts.text: Wait and think about it for a few days
opts.tip: Waiting gives attackers more time to delete or sell your data. Act fast.

steps.q: Thesis recovered! How did the ransomware get in? You remember clicking a Discord link from a stranger that said 'check out my game mod'. What should you have done?

opts.text: Never click unsolicited links, especially for downloads from strangers
opts.tip: Game mods and cracked software are one of the top ransomware delivery methods. Only download from official sources.

opts.text: It's fine as long as you have antivirus
opts.tip: Antivirus doesn't catch everything. New ransomware strains often bypass AV for days after release.

opts.text: Discord is safe, the problem was something else
opts.tip: Discord is a common ransomware delivery channel — attackers pose as gamers sharing files.

steps.q: Going forward, what's the smartest setup to protect your university work?

opts.text: Auto-save to cloud + regular USB backups kept unplugged when not in use
opts.tip: Unplugged USB drives can't be encrypted by ransomware. Cloud versioning provides a second layer. Both together = strong protection.

opts.text: Just remember to copy files manually sometimes
opts.tip: Manual copying gets forgotten. Automated backups are the only reliable approach.

opts.text: Store everything on the university network drive only
opts.tip: If ransomware reaches the university network (common!), those files get encrypted too.

scenarios.title: Small Business Ransomware
scenarios.banner: ALL BUSINESS DATA ENCRYPTED! Pay 5 BTC within 48 hours. Counter: 47:58:12

steps.q: Your small business's point-of-sale system and customer database are encrypted. It's Friday afternoon. First move?

opts.text: Pull the network cable / disconnect all systems from the internet
opts.tip: Physical disconnection is the fastest way to stop spread. Even if one system is lost, you can protect the others.

opts.text: Call your bank to transfer Bitcoin
opts.tip: Never pay without exhausting other options. Check for backups and contact authorities first.

opts.text: Post about it on social media to warn customers
opts.tip: Don't post publicly yet — notify only required authorities and legal counsel first to avoid legal liability.

steps.q: You find a backup hard drive your IT person set up last month. It was kept in a drawer, unplugged. Is this backup safe to use?

opts.text: Yes — unplugged drives can't be reached by ransomware
opts.tip: This is exactly why air-gapped (physically disconnected) backups are so valuable. Ransomware can't encrypt what it can't reach!

opts.text: No — all backups get infected automatically
opts.tip: Only connected storage gets encrypted. An unplugged drive is safe from network-based ransomware.

opts.text: Maybe — scan it with the infected computer to check
opts.tip: Never scan a potentially clean backup with an infected machine! Use a known-clean device instead.

steps.q: While restoring, your employee admits they clicked an email attachment labeled 'Invoice_Final.pdf.exe' two weeks ago. How long was the ransomware lurking?

opts.text: Likely 2 weeks — ransomware often waits before activating to maximize damage
opts.tip: Modern ransomware can sit dormant for weeks, quietly spreading through systems and deleting backup copies before activating. This is why monitoring is critical!

opts.text: Just a few hours — it would have activated immediately
opts.tip: Immediate activation is old-style ransomware. Modern variants wait to maximize infected systems and corrupt more backups.

opts.text: It started the moment they opened the file
opts.tip: The initial infection may have happened then, but activation (encryption) is often delayed strategically.

steps.q: Customer payment data may have been stolen. What's your legal obligation?

opts.text: Notify affected customers and relevant data protection authorities
opts.tip: Data breach notification is legally required in most countries. Customers have a right to know so they can protect their payment cards.

opts.text: Only tell customers if you're sure data was stolen
opts.tip: If there's reasonable suspicion of data theft, notification is typically required regardless of certainty.

opts.text: Wait until you have full details before notifying anyone
opts.tip: Waiting too long to notify violates most data protection laws and increases your legal liability.

steps.q: After this incident, what's the most cost-effective security improvement for a small business?

opts.text: Automated daily backups + staff phishing training + MFA on all accounts
opts.tip: These three measures prevent and survive the vast majority of ransomware attacks — and they're relatively affordable for small businesses.

opts.text: Hire a full-time security team
opts.tip: A full-time team is expensive for small businesses. The basics — backups, training, MFA — provide the most protection per dollar.

opts.text: Just buy cyber insurance and accept the risk
opts.tip: Insurance pays after the damage. Prevention is always cheaper — cyber insurance premiums also decrease with better security practices.

scenarios.title: Home User Ransomware
scenarios.banner: YOUR PHOTOS & DOCUMENTS ARE LOCKED! Pay 0.2 BTC. Your files will be deleted in 72 hours.

steps.q: Your home PC is hit. Family photos and tax documents are encrypted. What do you do first?

opts.text: Turn off WiFi and disconnect any external drives
opts.tip: Disconnect now! Family photos stored on a connected external drive or NAS can still be saved if you act fast.

opts.text: Google 'how to pay ransom' and pay immediately
opts.tip: Don't rush to pay. Many home ransomware variants have free decryption tools available — check first.

opts.text: Turn the computer off and leave it for a few days
opts.tip: Turning off may help stop active encryption, but 'waiting' loses valuable time for free decryption options.

steps.q: You check nomoreransom.org — the official free decryption tool site. They have a tool for your ransomware variant! What do you do?

opts.text: Download and run the official decryption tool on the infected PC
opts.tip: nomoreransom.org is run by Europol and law enforcement — completely legitimate. Free decryption tools work on many common ransomware variants!

opts.text: It's probably a scam — pay the ransom instead
opts.tip: nomoreransom.org is run by Europol! It's one of the most trusted cybersecurity resources available to the public.

opts.text: Search for random decryption tools on Google instead
opts.tip: Random tools from Google searches may be fake and install more malware. Always use nomoreransom.org first.

steps.q: The tool works! Files are restored. You realize the ransomware came from a cracked game download. What do you tell your family?

opts.text: Only download software from official stores or verified official websites
opts.tip: Cracked software is one of the top infection vectors. The 'free' game cost your family's memories. Official sources protect everyone.

opts.text: Just don't download games anymore
opts.tip: Games from official sources (Steam, App Store, etc.) are safe. The lesson is about source quality, not avoiding games.

opts.text: It was just bad luck — these things happen
opts.tip: It wasn't luck — cracked software knowingly bundles malware. Behavior change prevents recurrence.

steps.q: Your family photos weren't backed up. How devastated would you have been without the free tool? What do you set up now?

opts.text: Automatic photo backup to Google Photos / iCloud + a yearly external drive backup
opts.tip: Cloud auto-backup means photos are safe the moment they're taken. Yearly external backup covers documents. Two layers beat any ransomware.

opts.text: Just be more careful about what you download
opts.tip: Behavior change helps, but accidents happen. Backups ensure photos survive no matter what.

opts.text: Print out important photos
opts.tip: Physical prints help for some photos, but don't protect documents, and don't scale. Digital backups are essential.

steps.q: What free tool should EVERYONE know about for ransomware help?

opts.text: nomoreransom.org — free decryption tools from Europol and law enforcement
opts.tip: Share this with everyone! nomoreransom.org has helped millions of victims worldwide recover files for free. Bookmark it now!

opts.text: Just search 'ransomware removal' on Google
opts.tip: Google results include scam tools that charge money or install more malware. nomoreransom.org is the trusted official resource.

opts.text: There's no help available — you have to pay or lose files
opts.tip: This is exactly what attackers want you to believe! nomoreransom.org has decryption keys for hundreds of ransomware variants.

result.passed: Good detective work! You passed and earned 100 XP. 🌟

cases.title: The CEO's Leaked Photos
cases.intro: The CEO's private photos appeared online. HOW did the attacker get in?

clues.text: CEO posted birthday party. Caption mentions pet 'Buddy' and shows birthday cake with candles.
CEO's email password was 'Buddy2024' — pet name + birth year.
Login from foreign IP at 3 AM. CEO was asleep in Doha.
Two-factor authentication was DISABLED on CEO's email.

cases.question: What was the ROOT CAUSE of this breach?

options.text: Weak password based on public social media info + no MFA
The company's firewall was misconfigured
An employee shared the password deliberately

cases.lesson: The attacker found the pet name and birthday on social media, guessed the password, and logged in without any second factor. Never use personal info as passwords, and ALWAYS enable MFA!

cases.title: The Insider Data Leak
cases.intro: Classified files appeared on a competitor's website. WHO leaked them?

clues.text: User 'sara.m' accessed classified folder 127 times in 2 weeks — 10x above normal.
Personal phone connected via USB to sara.m's PC with file transfer 23 times.
sara.m sent 3 emails to personal Gmail with large attachments before the leak.
sara.m resigned 5 days after the leak. She's joining the competitor.

cases.question: What's the most likely explanation?

options.text: Sara copied files to personal devices before joining a competitor
An external hacker broke into the network
The files were accidentally made public

cases.lesson: Insider threats are real! Companies need access monitoring, USB device policies, and Data Loss Prevention tools. Watch for unusual access spikes from departing employees.

cases.title: The Phishing Finance Attack
cases.intro: QAR 200,000 was transferred to an unknown account. How did this happen?

clues.text: Finance team received email from 'ceo@cornpany.com' (note the 'rn' instead of 'm').
Finance officer initiated urgent transfer within 20 minutes of receiving the email.
No phone verification was made with the CEO before the transfer.
cornpany.com was registered 3 days before the attack from an anonymous registrar.

cases.question: What type of attack was this?

options.text: Business Email Compromise (BEC) — fake CEO email with a lookalike domain
The CEO's account was hacked and used directly
An insider in finance stole the money

cases.lesson: BEC attacks use lookalike domains to impersonate executives. Always verify large transfers by phone, check sender domains carefully, and implement a dual-approval policy for wire transfers.

cases.title: The Ransomware Hospital Attack
cases.intro: A hospital's systems are locked and patient records are inaccessible. What happened?

clues.text: IT staff member opened an attachment named 'Invoice_March.exe' from an unknown sender.
Within 10 minutes of opening the file, 47,000 files were encrypted across the network.
Last backup was 6 months ago. Backup system was on the same network segment.
Attackers demand 50 Bitcoin within 72 hours or files will be permanently deleted.

cases.question: What combination of failures made this attack so devastating?

options.text: Phishing email opened + outdated backups on same network = full encryption with no recovery
The hospital's firewall was too expensive to maintain
A former employee planted the malware physically

cases.lesson: Ransomware spreads fast once inside. The real damage came from stale backups on the same network. Backups must be isolated (air-gapped), tested regularly, and staff must be trained to never open executable attachments.

cases.title: The Student Account Takeover
cases.intro: A university student's account was used to submit fake assignment extensions and access exam papers early. How?

clues.text: Student received an email from 'portal-qu.support.com' asking to re-verify login credentials.
Student clicked the link and entered their username and password on the fake page.
portal-qu.support.com is NOT the official university domain (qu.edu.qa). Registered yesterday.
Account was accessed from an IP in Eastern Europe 4 minutes after the student entered credentials.

cases.question: What attack technique was used?

options.text: Spear phishing — a targeted fake login page designed to steal student credentials
The university's database was hacked directly
The student's roommate guessed the password

cases.lesson: Spear phishing targets specific individuals with convincing fake pages. Always check the URL before entering credentials — official portals use .edu.qa domains, never third-party domains. Enable MFA on all academic accounts!

cases.title: The Supply Chain Attack
cases.intro: Thousands of companies were breached simultaneously through software they all use. How?

clues.text: All affected companies installed a routine update for 'NetManager Pro' on the same day.
The update package contained a hidden backdoor that contacted external servers after installation.
The update was digitally signed with the vendor's legitimate certificate — it looked authentic.
Investigation revealed the software vendor's build server was compromised 3 months earlier.

cases.question: What type of attack affected thousands of companies at once?

options.text: Supply chain attack — malware injected into a legitimate software update
Each company was individually targeted by the same hacker group
A zero-day vulnerability in all companies' firewalls was exploited

cases.lesson: Supply chain attacks are devastating because victims trust legitimate vendors. The SolarWinds attack used exactly this method. Verify software integrity with checksums, monitor network behavior after updates, and use zero-trust architecture.

cases.title: The WiFi Café Attack
cases.intro: Several café customers had their banking sessions hijacked. What happened?

clues.text: Two networks named 'CaféWiFi_Free' existed — one legitimate, one set up by an attacker at a nearby table.
Victims' devices automatically connected to the stronger (fake) signal without prompting.
Victims' banking session tokens were captured and replayed by the attacker's device.
A laptop running Kali Linux with a WiFi adapter was found abandoned at a corner table.

cases.question: What attack technique was used at the café?

options.text: Evil Twin attack — a rogue WiFi hotspot intercepting traffic via man-in-the-middle
The café's router was hacked remotely from the internet
Customers' devices had pre-installed spyware

cases.lesson: Evil Twin attacks create fake hotspots with familiar names. Always use a VPN on public WiFi, verify network names with staff, and ensure websites use HTTPS before entering credentials.

cases.title: The Cloud Storage Breach
cases.intro: A company's confidential documents were found publicly accessible online. How were they exposed?

clues.text: An S3 bucket named 'company-internal-docs' was configured as 'Public' instead of 'Private'.
The bucket had been publicly accessible for 47 days. 2,300 unique IPs accessed it.
A developer changed permissions to 'Public' while testing and forgot to revert the setting.
A security researcher found the bucket via a tool that automatically scans for misconfigured cloud storage.

cases.question: What was the root cause of this cloud data exposure?

options.text: Misconfigured cloud storage permissions left public by accident
A hacker exploited a vulnerability in Amazon's servers
An employee intentionally published the documents

cases.lesson: Misconfigured cloud storage is one of the most common breach causes. Use automated configuration scanning, enforce least-privilege access, and audit permissions regularly.

cases.title: The Password Spray Attack
cases.intro: Dozens of employee accounts were compromised in one morning with no alert triggering. How?

clues.text: Each compromised account had exactly 3 failed attempts before success — just under the 5-attempt lockout threshold.
Failed attempts were spread across 6 hours, one attempt per account every 12 minutes.
All compromised accounts used one of the top 10 most common passwords: 'Company2024', 'Welcome1!'.
Attempts came from 47 different IP addresses across multiple countries — a botnet.

cases.question: What attack technique bypassed the lockout policy?

options.text: Password spraying — trying common passwords slowly across many accounts to avoid lockouts
Brute force — rapidly trying all combinations on one account
Credential stuffing — using passwords leaked from another site

cases.lesson: Password spraying tries a FEW common passwords across MANY accounts slowly to evade lockouts. Defenses: ban common passwords, use MFA, monitor for distributed low-rate login attempts, and implement IP reputation filtering.

HackLab.title: Hack Lab
HackLab.questionLabel: What attack would a real hacker choose?
HackLab.timeoutLesson: Time's up! In real attacks, speed matters.

intro.line1: Time to think like an attacker! Understanding how hackers think is the BEST way to build stronger defenses.
intro.line2: You'll see a target with a specific weakness. Choose the attack a real hacker would use.
intro.line3: Each correct answer teaches you what defense would have prevented it. Know your enemy!

result.title: Lab Complete
result.perfect: You think like a pro hacker — which makes you a pro defender! +100 XP earned! 🏆
result.passed: Good offensive instincts! You passed and earned 100 XP. 🌟
result.failed: Almost! Score {passingScore} points to earn XP. Understanding attackers is key to defense.

missions.target: A company employee who loves clicking email links

opts.text: Send a phishing email with a fake invoice
opts.lesson: Phishing targets human behavior. Without training, employees click dangerous links. Security awareness training is critical!

opts.text: Brute force their password
opts.lesson: Brute forcing takes time and triggers alerts. Phishing is faster and exploits the weakest link: human trust.

opts.text: DDoS the company website
opts.lesson: DDoS disrupts service but doesn't steal data. Phishing gives you direct access to the network.

missions.target: A server running software from 2019 (never updated)

opts.text: Exploit a known vulnerability (CVE)
opts.lesson: Unpatched software has known exploits. This is why updates are critical — attackers scan for exactly these gaps!

opts.text: Social engineer the admin
opts.lesson: Why bother when there's a known exploit? Attackers always take the path of least resistance.

opts.text: Physically break into the server room
opts.lesson: Physical attacks are risky when remote exploits for unpatched systems already exist.

opts.text: Credential stuffing / brute force
opts.lesson: Weak passwords fall to automated attacks in seconds. 'password123' is in every breach database. MFA + strong passwords are essential!

opts.text: Phishing for their password
opts.lesson: With such a weak password, automated cracking is faster and doesn't require the victim's interaction.

opts.text: Encrypt their files with ransomware
opts.lesson: You need access first! Ransomware comes AFTER initial access. Start with the weak password.

missions.defense: No backup system, no DR plan

opts.text: Deploy ransomware after gaining access
opts.lesson: No backups means the victim MUST pay to recover data. This is why the 3-2-1 backup rule exists!

opts.text: Steal data and sell it
opts.lesson: Data theft works, but ransomware against a backup-less company guarantees payment.

opts.text: Deface their website
opts.lesson: Website defacement is embarrassing but low-profit. Ransomware is devastating.

missions.target: Open WiFi at a popular café in Souq Waqif

opts.text: Set up a fake WiFi hotspot (Evil Twin)
opts.lesson: Evil Twin attacks create a fake network. When people connect, all traffic flows through you. Always use VPN on public WiFi!

opts.text: Hack the café's router
opts.lesson: Why hack the router when you can create your own fake network? Evil Twin is easier and invisible.

opts.text: Physically steal someone's laptop
opts.lesson: Physical theft is obvious and risky. Digital WiFi attacks are invisible and scalable.

missions.target: A network with no monitoring or logging

opts.text: Move laterally through the network slowly
opts.lesson: Without monitoring, attackers can explore undetected for months. 24/7 monitoring detects this 'living off the land' behavior!

opts.text: Launch a loud, fast attack
opts.lesson: Even without monitoring, loud attacks can trigger other defenses. Smart attackers are patient.

opts.text: Attack from outside the network
opts.lesson: If you're already inside via phishing, lateral movement is far more valuable than external attacks.

missions.target: A hospital with patient records on an old Windows XP machine

opts.text: Exploit EternalBlue (MS17-010) to spread ransomware
opts.lesson: WannaCry devastated hospitals using exactly this exploit. Legacy systems MUST be air-gapped or upgraded — they're ticking time bombs!

opts.text: Phish a nurse for her credentials
opts.lesson: Phishing works, but a known remote exploit on an unpatched OS is faster and requires no victim interaction.

opts.text: Bribe an IT employee
opts.lesson: Insider threats exist, but a public exploit is more reliable and leaves less evidence.

missions.target: A login page with no rate limiting

opts.text: Automate thousands of password attempts
opts.lesson: Without rate limiting or lockouts, attackers can try millions of passwords per second. Always implement account lockout and CAPTCHA!

opts.text: Try social engineering the help desk
opts.lesson: Social engineering works, but automation is faster when there's no rate limiting in place.

opts.text: Intercept the login traffic
opts.lesson: Traffic interception requires a man-in-the-middle position. Brute force is simpler here.

missions.target: A developer who committed API keys to a public GitHub repo

opts.text: Scan GitHub for leaked keys and use them immediately
opts.lesson: Bots scan GitHub 24/7 for API keys. A leaked key can be exploited within minutes of being pushed. Use environment variables and secrets managers!

opts.text: Hack the developer's laptop
opts.lesson: Why hack a laptop when the keys are already public? Attackers always take the easiest path.

opts.text: DDoS the company API
opts.lesson: DDoS denies service but doesn't give you access. A leaked API key gives full authenticated access.

missions.target: An IoT security camera with default credentials

opts.text: Try admin/admin or admin/password to log in
opts.lesson: Millions of IoT devices ship with default credentials that users never change. Always change defaults immediately — attackers use automated scanners to find them!

opts.text: Exploit a buffer overflow in the firmware
opts.lesson: Firmware exploits exist, but trying default credentials takes 5 seconds. Attackers always try the easy path first.

opts.text: Intercept the camera's video stream
opts.lesson: Intercepting requires network access you don't have yet. Default credentials give you direct admin access.

missions.target: An executive who reuses the same password everywhere

opts.text: Find their password in a previous data breach dump
opts.lesson: Credential reuse is rampant. Attackers buy breach databases and test credentials across multiple services. A password manager prevents this!

opts.text: Physically watch them type their password
opts.lesson: Shoulder surfing is risky and impractical. Breach databases give you the password without being anywhere near the victim.

opts.text: Send them a malware attachment
opts.lesson: Malware works, but if you already know their reused password from a breach, you don't need malware.

missions.target: A company that allows USB drives from anywhere
missions.defense: No USB policy, no endpoint protection

opts.text: Drop a malware-infected USB in the parking lot
opts.lesson: The 'USB drop attack' works because curious people plug in found drives. Organizations must block unknown USB devices and train employees!

opts.text: Email a phishing link to all employees
opts.lesson: Phishing works, but physical USB drops have historically high success rates — people are naturally curious.

opts.text: Remotely exploit their VPN
opts.lesson: VPN exploits require a known vulnerability. A USB drop attack bypasses all network defenses completely.

SmartTrap.title: Smart Trap
SmartTrap.timeoutTip: Time's up! In a real attack, hesitation costs you.

intro.line1: Welcome to the smartest apartment in Doha! Fridge, speaker, camera, door lock, printer — everything is connected.
intro.line2: The previous tenant left every IoT device with its default settings. Your job: move room to room and fix each vulnerability.
intro.line3: Read the situation carefully — there's always a best fix, not just any fix. Speed earns bonus points!

result.title: Apartment Secured
result.perfect: IoT expert! Every device in your smart home is locked down. +100 XP earned! 🏆
result.passed: Good work! Most devices secured. You passed and earned 100 XP. 🌟
result.failed: Almost! Score {passingScore} points to earn XP. Default passwords and open access are the biggest IoT risks!

rooms.puzzle: The smart fridge display shows: 'Admin login — Password: admin'. What's the risk and what do you do?

opts.text: Change the default password to something strong and unique
opts.tip: Default passwords like 'admin' are the #1 IoT vulnerability. Attackers scan the internet for these automatically — millions of devices are compromised this way!

opts.text: It's just a fridge, no sensitive data is at risk
opts.tip: A hacked fridge can be a gateway to your ENTIRE home network! Attackers use it as a stepping stone to reach laptops, phones, and cameras.

opts.text: Disconnect it from WiFi entirely
opts.tip: Disconnecting stops the immediate risk but loses all smart features. The right fix is securing it with a strong password.

rooms.puzzle: Your smart speaker responds to ANY voice — including from outside through the window, from TV ads, and from guests. What's the best fix?

opts.text: Enable voice recognition so it only responds to registered voices
opts.tip: Voice matching prevents strangers, recordings, and even YouTube videos from controlling your devices or accessing your calendar and smart home!

opts.text: Turn it off whenever you leave home
opts.tip: This helps when you're away but doesn't fix the vulnerability when you're home. Anyone in the room can still issue commands.

opts.text: Move it away from windows
opts.tip: WiFi and Bluetooth aren't affected by window placement, and voice commands can come from TV, guests, or anyone in earshot.

rooms.puzzle: You discover your bedroom security camera is accessible from the internet with no password — it even appears on Shodan.io, a search engine for exposed devices. What do you do?

opts.text: Set a strong password AND disable remote access unless you specifically need it
opts.tip: Unsecured cameras are indexed by Shodan and can be watched by anyone in the world. Strong password + disabled remote access = private again. Only enable remote access when needed.

opts.text: Point the camera at the wall so nothing sensitive is visible
opts.tip: The camera is still fully accessible on the internet! An attacker can also use its microphone, or pivot through it to attack other devices on your network.

opts.text: Cover the lens with tape
opts.tip: Tape blocks the video but not the microphone, and the camera remains a live entry point into your home network. Secure it properly!

rooms.puzzle: Your smart lock unlocks via Bluetooth from up to 30 meters away — meaning an attacker standing on the street could potentially unlock your door. What's the fix?

opts.text: Reduce Bluetooth range in settings and require a PIN code to unlock
opts.tip: Shorter range (under 5m) + mandatory PIN = two-factor security for your physical door. Bluetooth alone can be relayed and spoofed — always add a second factor!

opts.text: Keep a traditional key as backup and stop using the smart features
opts.tip: A physical key backup is wise, but it doesn't fix the Bluetooth vulnerability. The smart lock still responds to relay attacks from the street.

opts.text: Disable the smart features entirely and use it as a regular lock
opts.tip: That solves the problem but loses all smart features. It's better to configure it securely — range limits + PIN authentication gives you both security and convenience.

rooms.puzzle: Your network printer has WiFi Direct enabled, creating its own open hotspot. Anyone nearby can connect directly to it, print documents, and potentially use it as a network entry point.

opts.text: Disable WiFi Direct and connect the printer via your secure home network only
opts.tip: WiFi Direct creates an unprotected second entry point into your environment. Disabling it and using your secured home network closes this gap completely!

opts.text: The printer only has paper — there's no real security risk
opts.tip: Printers store copies of EVERYTHING you print in their memory. They can also be used as network pivots to attack other devices. Printers are a serious attack vector!

opts.text: Hide the printer in a closet or cabinet
opts.tip: WiFi signals pass through walls with ease! Physical hiding does nothing to stop a digital WiFi attack from a neighbor or someone parked outside.

rooms.puzzle: Your garden's smart irrigation app hasn't been updated in 2 years and uses HTTP (not HTTPS) to send commands. What should you fix?

opts.text: Update the app and firmware, and only use the system via HTTPS or a VPN
opts.tip: HTTP traffic is unencrypted — anyone on your WiFi or ISP can see and modify commands. Updates patch known vulnerabilities. Always prefer HTTPS for IoT apps!

opts.text: It controls water — worst case the garden gets overwatered
opts.tip: An attacker who owns your irrigation controller also has a foothold on your home network. Plus, overwatering can cause real property damage!

opts.text: Switch back to manual watering to avoid the risk
opts.tip: That avoids the issue but at the cost of the feature. The right approach is to secure the system — update it and use encrypted communications.

rooms.puzzle: Your baby monitor streams video over the internet using the manufacturer's cloud. You've never changed the default PIN '0000'. Who else might be watching?

opts.text: Change the PIN immediately and enable two-factor authentication on the account
opts.tip: Baby monitors with default PINs are actively targeted — there are websites that stream stolen baby monitor feeds. Change default credentials on ANY camera immediately!

opts.text: The baby monitor is encrypted — default PIN is fine
opts.tip: Encryption protects data in transit, but a default PIN means anyone who finds your device online can authenticate and watch. Encryption ≠ authentication security!

opts.text: Disconnect it from the internet and only use local viewing
opts.tip: Local-only viewing is safe but loses remote monitoring features. A better balance is securing the account properly with a strong PIN + 2FA.

rooms.puzzle: Your smart garage door opener app logs show 3 failed login attempts from an IP in another country at 2AM. What do you do?

opts.text: Change the password, enable 2FA, and review which devices have access
opts.tip: Failed login attempts from foreign IPs are a clear sign of a brute-force attack in progress. Changing password + 2FA stops it cold. Reviewing connected devices ensures no unauthorized access already exists.

opts.text: It's probably just a glitch — ignore it
opts.tip: Three failed attempts from an overseas IP at 2AM is NOT a glitch. It's a textbook brute-force attack. Ignoring it means they keep trying until they succeed.

opts.text: Disconnect the garage from smart features
opts.tip: That stops the attack but loses your smart features. Securing the account with 2FA is the better approach — it makes brute force practically impossible.
